19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Ludwig Wittgenstein

1889 – 1951

Mind as lived expression, not a private object hidden behind behavior.

The strongest reconstruction is this: Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind is a grammatical, expressive, embodied, anti-Cartesian, non-reductive philosophy of persons. It is not a “theory” of mind in the explanatory-scientific sense. It is a disciplined attempt to dissolve pictures that make us mislocate the mind: pictures of private inner objects, a hidden mental theatre, an occult ego, or a soul-substance standing behind the body. Yet he is not a behaviorist, not an eliminativist, and not a crude naturalist. He does not deny pain, thought, consciousness, religious seriousness, or the depth of “soul” language. His distinctive claim is that we misunderstand these things when we detach them from the grammar of human life: expression, training, criteria, practices, gestures, bodies, forms of life, and our attitudes toward one another.

A compressed formula would be: the mind is not an inner object; consciousness is not a private possession inspected by an inner eye; the soul is not a hidden thing behind the body, but the human being under the aspect of expressive, ethical, and sometimes religious life. This should not be read as reduction. It is closer to: the body of a living person is not a mere container of the soul, nor merely evidence for it; it is the field in which the soul is expressed. Wittgenstein’s later remark that “the human body is the best picture of the human soul” is therefore not ornamental. It is one of the most important statements for extracting his philosophy of mind. In the same cluster of remarks, he says that his attitude toward another human being is an “attitude toward a soul,” not an empirical opinion that the person has one.

There is an apparent paradox at the center of this report: Wittgenstein attacks many philosophical uses of “soul,” “mind,” and “inner process,” but the attack is not a dismissal of mindedness or spirituality. He attacks a misleading grammar. Theology matters here because religious language often operates precisely where the scientific picture tempts us either to literalize everything into quasi-empirical hypotheses or to dismiss everything as meaningless decoration. Wittgenstein’s own comments on God, ethics, immortality, prayer, guilt, and the soul show that he takes religious pictures seriously while refusing to assimilate them to natural science.

1. The governing problem: the inner-object picture

Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind is organized around a recurring diagnosis: philosophers are bewitched by the grammar of nouns and by the model of naming. Because “pain,” “thought,” “image,” “intention,” “soul,” and “consciousness” look like names, we are tempted to ask: what objects do they name? If not public objects, then private ones. If private ones, perhaps only the owner can really know them. If only the owner can know them, then other minds become doubtful and the self becomes a hidden subject.

His later work tries to break that sequence. The meaning of psychological vocabulary is not secured by pointing inward to private objects. It is learned and used within shared practices. The grammar of sensation words, belief words, intention words, and religious words is embedded in forms of life, not derived from a private act of inner ostension. This is why the private-language argument is so central. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes the target as the idea of a language whose words refer to immediate private sensations and are necessarily intelligible only to the speaker; it also notes that Wittgenstein’s argument is not a single proof but a complex set of remarks concentrated around Philosophical Investigations §§243–271.

The result is not that pain is unreal, nor that consciousness is only behavior. Wittgenstein’s famous corrective is that the sensation is “not a something, but not a nothing either.” His point is that a philosophical picture has forced us into a false choice: either a mental item must be an inner object, or it must be unreal. Wittgenstein rejects that grammar.

2. Early Wittgenstein: the subject, the limit of the world, and the unsayable

The Tractatus is not a philosophy of mind in the later ordinary-language sense, but it contains decisive claims about the self, consciousness, solipsism, ethics, God, and immortality. These early claims remain important because they show Wittgenstein’s lifelong hostility to treating the subject as one more object in the world.

In the Tractatus, the world is the totality of facts, not things. Propositions picture facts by sharing logical form with them. This picture-theory setting leads Wittgenstein to exclude many things from factual representation: logic, ethics, aesthetics, the metaphysical subject, and God are not items among the facts. The SEP summary of the Tractatus notes this central architecture: world, thought, and language share logical form; propositions are truth-functional; and what lies outside factual discourse is not expressible as another fact.

The early self is especially important. Wittgenstein says that “the thinking, presenting subject” is not found in the world, and that the metaphysical subject is not in the world but is the limit of the world. He compares the subject to the eye in relation to the visual field: the eye is not seen inside the field as one more object. The body, by contrast, can appear in the world like other objects. This distinction between the empirical body and the metaphysical subject is one of the deepest early sources of his later anti-Cartesianism.

This is not Cartesian dualism. Descartes makes the thinking subject a kind of substance. Wittgenstein does something stranger: he says that the subject cannot be represented as a worldly entity at all. Solipsism, he says, contains a truth, but it cannot be stated; it shows itself. “I am my world” does not mean that an empirical ego owns all facts. It means that the possibility of a world-for-me is not itself another fact in the world.

The theological and existential remarks in the Tractatus belong to this same structure. God does not reveal himself as an item within the world; the mystical is not how the world is, but that the world is. Wittgenstein also denies that temporal immortality of the soul solves the riddle of life: survival through endless time would not answer the real problem. The solution to the riddle of life in space and time lies outside space and time.

This has direct consequences for philosophy of mind. Early Wittgenstein does not give us a psychology of consciousness. He blocks the idea that the subject of consciousness could be discovered as an object. The “I” that makes representation possible is not a mental thing. The early view therefore already rejects both crude materialism and Cartesian introspectionism: the subject is not a brain-state, but neither is it an immaterial object accessible by inner perception.

The weakness of the early position, from the later perspective, is that it still speaks in an austere metaphysical register: limit, world, subject, showing, saying. Later Wittgenstein will not so much refute this as rework it. The metaphysical subject becomes the grammatical “I”; the unsayable becomes the misuse of language; the limit of the world becomes the limit imposed by our practices and forms of life.

3. The transitional critique: “I,” mind, and the illusion of a bodiless ego

The Blue Book marks a decisive transition. Wittgenstein begins to treat philosophical confusion about the mind as a grammatical confusion about words such as “I,” “mind,” “seeing,” “feeling,” and “thinking.” He notes that some uses of “I,” especially as subject, can create the illusion that the word refers to a bodiless entity seated inside the body. But the cure is not to deny the mind; it is to examine the use of the relevant words. He explicitly says that the word “mind” has a meaning, that is, a use, but that philosophical work is needed to understand what that use is.

This is important because it defeats two opposite misreadings. On one side, Wittgenstein is not saying that “mind” is meaningless. On the other side, he is not saying that “mind” names an inner object. The word has use, but the use is not the naming of a private entity.

The Blue Book also criticizes sense-data language. Philosophers invent phrases like “sense-datum” to solve puzzles about appearance and reality, but the new phrase can make it seem as if we have discovered a new kind of entity. Wittgenstein’s point is not that visual experience is unreal. It is that philosophical notation can manufacture metaphysics. The appearance of an inner object is often produced by a grammatical analogy: because “chair” names a public object, “sensation” must name a private object.

This transitional work gives us a core principle for the later philosophy of mind: psychological concepts are not to be explained by introspective metaphysics but by their grammar. We must ask how words are learned, when they are used, what counts as their correct or incorrect application, what they express, and how they connect with human action.

4. Later Wittgenstein: mind as grammar, expression, and form of life

The later Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind is concentrated in the Philosophical Investigations and the philosophy-of-psychology writings. Its central movement is away from mental objects and toward public criteria, expression, and human life.

4.1 Form of life and the ground of mental language

Wittgenstein’s later philosophy begins from use. A word’s meaning is not an occult object accompanying it; it is its role in language. But language itself is not merely a calculus. It is woven into activities. This is why “form of life” matters.

In the Investigations, Wittgenstein says that agreement in language is not merely agreement in opinions but in form of life; communication requires agreement not only in definitions but also in judgments.

This matters for philosophy of mind because psychological concepts depend on shared patterns of response. We do not first identify private objects and then attach names to them. We are trained into ways of reacting, comforting, reporting, correcting, doubting, pretending, hiding, confessing, praying, joking, and explaining. Pain, belief, expectation, fear, remorse, joy, and hope have their home in such practices.

This does not mean that the community arbitrarily decides what pain is. It means that the concept of pain is not intelligible outside a human form of life in which pain has characteristic expressions, circumstances, responses, and consequences.

4.2 The private-language argument and sensation

The private-language argument is not merely an argument about language. It is a critique of a whole conception of consciousness. The tempting picture is: I have a private sensation; I inwardly point to it; I name it; the word’s meaning is fixed by this private act. Wittgenstein’s response is that naming a sensation already presupposes a grammar. A mere inner association does not establish a standard of correctness.

His diary example makes the point vivid. Suppose someone writes “S” every time he has a certain private sensation. The problem is not that others cannot verify it. The deeper problem is that, if there is no criterion of correctness, then whatever seems right will count as right, and that means there is no genuine distinction between correct and incorrect use.

This is the philosophical core. A word does not acquire meaning merely because an inner episode accompanies it. Meaning requires a place in a practice, including standards of use. The concept of a necessarily private language collapses because it imagines meaning without the possibility of normativity.

But again, this is not a denial of sensation. Wittgenstein insists that the philosophical trouble arises because we have forced ourselves into the model of a private object. The sensation is not a hidden thing, but it is not nothing. The word “pain” has a grammar; the grammar is not the grammar of naming an object hidden behind behavior.

The consequence for consciousness is radical. Consciousness is not a private inner realm whose contents are first given and then linguistically encoded. It is not an epistemic chamber. The grammar of conscious states is inseparable from human expression, action, and criteria.

4.3 Pain, expression, and the person as subject

Wittgenstein’s treatment of pain is the template for much of his philosophy of mind. Pain is not inferred from behavior as a hidden cause in the way smoke indicates fire. Pain-behavior is often an expression of pain. A cry, a wince, a withdrawal, a plea, a grimace, a request for help, and the sentence “I am in pain” do not all function as reports from an inner theatre.

This is why Wittgenstein says that pain-behavior points to the painful place, but the subject of pain is the person who gives expression to it. The subject is not an inner item called “the mind”; it is the human being.

That claim is easy to underestimate. It means that philosophical attempts to locate pain “inside” consciousness have already abstracted pain from its grammar. A person is in pain. A body can be injured. A face can express suffering. A cry can be pain-behavior. But the subject of pain is not a Cartesian ego hovering behind these phenomena.

This also shows why Wittgenstein is not a behaviorist. Behaviorism says, roughly, that mental states are reducible to behavior or behavioral dispositions. Wittgenstein’s view is subtler: behavior can be expression, criterion, context, or manifestation, but not a mere substitute for the mental. He does not say that pain is just pain-behavior. He says that our concept of pain is not the concept of a private object logically independent of all possible expression.

4.4 Consciousness and the living body

One of Wittgenstein’s most important remarks for consciousness is that only of a living human being, and of what resembles one, can we say that it has sensations, sees, is blind, hears, is deaf, is conscious, or unconscious.

This is not a biological theory of consciousness in the modern neuroscientific sense. It is a grammatical observation. Our concepts of consciousness are anchored in the life of organisms, especially human beings: waking, sleeping, attending, responding, suffering, speaking, recognizing, failing to recognize, acting intentionally, being startled, fainting, recovering, deceiving, pretending.

This does not settle every question about animals, machines, or artificial intelligence. It does say that consciousness-ascription is not a matter of peering behind behavior to inspect an occult object. The application of consciousness concepts depends on patterns of life and response. Where those patterns are absent, the grammar becomes strained. Where analogues are present, new language-games may develop.

Wittgenstein’s question “How can a body have a soul?” is therefore not a request for a hidden mechanism. It is a way of exposing the oddity of treating body and soul as two separable objects that then need to be externally glued together.

4.5 First-person authority: avowal, not inner observation

Wittgenstein’s view of first-person psychological statements is one of his greatest contributions. When I say “I am in pain,” I am usually not reporting an inner observation. I do not look inward, identify a private object, and then describe it. The utterance is closer to an expression of pain, though it is linguistically articulated and can have many roles.

This explains first-person authority without Cartesian privacy. I do not normally know my pain by evidence. I express it. The asymmetry between “I am in pain” and “He is in pain” is not that I perceive a private object while others infer it indirectly. The asymmetry lies in grammar: first-person present-tense psychological utterances often function as avowals, not observational reports.

Wittgenstein also warns against exaggerating the gap between self and others. He asks us to consider real cases of doubt about another person’s pain or fear, rather than an abstract philosophical fantasy. In actual life, doubt has conditions. It is not the default relation to other human beings.

This leads to a powerful anti-skeptical point: the “problem of other minds” is not solved by an argument from analogy, because the problem is badly framed. Other people are not normally hypotheses. They are encountered as expressive beings. The skeptical picture first strips away the ordinary criteria of mindedness and then asks how mindedness can be recovered.

4.6 Understanding, thinking, belief, expectation

Wittgenstein applies the same method to understanding, thinking, belief, expectation, and intention. The philosophical temptation is to say: understanding must be an inner process; belief must be a mental state hidden behind behavior; intending must be an inner act that gives meaning to outward movement.

He resists this. In the Investigations he tells us not to think of understanding as a mental process in the sense in which pain increasing, hearing a tune, or hearing a sentence may be mental processes.

This does not mean that there are no experiences of understanding. Of course there are moments of dawning comprehension, images, inner words, feelings of confidence, and so on. But none of these is the essence of understanding. Someone understands a rule, a word, a proof, or an instruction by being able to go on, apply it, correct mistakes, explain, respond, and participate in a practice.

Belief is treated similarly. Wittgenstein says we should look at the consequences of belief, where it takes us, and what it makes us do. He adds that an inner process stands in need of outward criteria.

This is crucial for a comprehensive philosophy of mind. The mental is not denied, but psychological verbs are not decoded by identifying private episodes. Their grammar is normative, practical, expressive, and context-sensitive.

Expectation and hope are especially revealing. An expectation is not simply a feeling hovering in consciousness. Its significance depends on the surroundings: what is being waited for, what would count as fulfillment, what actions follow, what would disappoint it. Hope is not just a sensation; it is a phenomenon of human life.

4.7 The soul: not a hidden object, not a dispensable metaphor

Wittgenstein’s remarks on the soul are easily misread. On one reading, he abolishes the soul by reducing everything to behavior. On another, he preserves the soul as a mysterious inner substance. Both readings miss his grammar.

He says that only of what behaves like a human being can one say that it has pains; one has to say it of a body, or of a soul which some body has. The formulation is deliberately unsettling: if we say the soul has pain, how does this relate to the body? If we say the body has pain, how can a body have a soul?

The later answer is not a metaphysical theory but a change of aspect. A human being is not a body plus an invisible object. Nor is a human being merely a body-machine. The living human body is expressive. It is the presence of a person. That is why Wittgenstein can say that the human body is the best picture of the human soul.

The “soul” here names, or better discloses, the personal aspect under which we encounter human beings: suffering, joy, shame, courage, love, duplicity, prayer, remorse, tenderness, cruelty, confession, dignity. It is not an empirical posit. It is bound up with how we respond to persons.

This is why Wittgenstein’s remark about not believing someone is an automaton matters. He says that “I believe he is not an automaton” so far makes no sense; his attitude toward the other is an attitude toward a soul, not the opinion that the other has one.

That remark may be the deepest point in Wittgenstein’s philosophy of other minds. The other person’s mindedness is not normally a theory I hold. It is manifest in the form of my relation to them. To treat someone as a soul is not to infer a ghost inside the body; it is to inhabit a human attitude.

5. Theology is not a distraction

Wittgenstein’s theological remarks should not be quarantined from his philosophy of mind. They illuminate how pictures, expressions, and forms of life can be serious without functioning as empirical theories.

5.1 Ethics, God, and the limits of factual discourse

In the Lecture on Ethics, Wittgenstein says ethics concerns absolute value, the meaning of life, and the right way of living. He argues that no statement of fact can imply absolute value, and that a complete scientific description of the world would still contain only facts, not ethics.

This is continuous with the Tractatus. If God, value, and the mystical are not facts in the world, then attempts to state them as facts will misfire. But Wittgenstein does not treat this misfire as trivial. He speaks of experiences such as wonder at the existence of the world and absolute safety. He then says that when these are expressed in ordinary factual language, the expressions are nonsensical or involve misuse of language.

The key is that “nonsense” here is not mere rubbish. Wittgenstein says that ethical and religious expressions show a tendency to run against the limits of language, and that he deeply respects this tendency.

For philosophy of mind, this means that certain dimensions of subjectivity—wonder, guilt, absolute responsibility, religious fear, grace, prayer—cannot be reduced to empirical psychology without loss. They also cannot be turned into factual metaphysics without distortion. They belong to the human form of life at a limit.

5.2 Religious pictures: creation, guilt, safety, soul

In the Lecture on Ethics, Wittgenstein connects religious expressions to experiences: “God created the world” corresponds to wonder at the world’s existence; being safe in the hands of God corresponds to absolute safety; guilt is described as God’s disapproval. He also notes that when one tries to drop the simile, no ordinary fact remains behind it.

This is often read as non-cognitivism: religious language expresses attitudes but does not state truths. That reading is too simple. Wittgenstein’s position is better understood as a refusal to assimilate religious language either to scientific hypothesis or to mere emotional noise. Religious utterances can be life-governing, world-structuring, penitential, expressive, ritual, confessional, and grammatical.

Recent scholarship still debates this. A Cambridge Element on Wittgenstein and religious belief notes that Wittgenstein published almost nothing on religion, yet his concept of religious belief has been influential and contested; it also criticizes labels such as fideist, non-cognitivist, and relativist as misleading. A 2026 article on Wittgenstein and cognitivism similarly frames the question as a live dispute, distinguishing stronger and weaker forms of religious non-cognitivism from cognitivist readings.

The important point for the soul is this: theological soul-language need not be treated as a defective scientific theory about a ghostly substance. It may be a picture through which human life is understood, judged, confessed, transformed, or redeemed. That does not make it decorative. For Wittgenstein, pictures can govern a life.

5.3 Immortality and the soul after death

The Tractatus rejects the idea that temporal survival solves the problem of life. An endless continuation of the soul through time would not solve the riddle; the answer lies outside space and time.

Later, however, Wittgenstein does not simply dismiss religious teaching about the soul’s survival. In the Investigations, he says that religion teaches that the soul can exist when the body has disintegrated, and he adds that he understands this teaching and can imagine plenty in connection with it. He also says that pictures may do the same service as words, and that the point is the service they perform.

This is a striking passage. Wittgenstein is not offering a proof of immortality. Nor is he treating the doctrine as meaningless nonsense. He is asking what role the doctrine and its pictures play. What work do they do in confession, fear, hope, mourning, judgment, repentance, worship, or moral seriousness?

A Wittgensteinian theology of the soul would therefore avoid two reductions. It would not reduce the soul to a scientifically detectable entity. It would also not reduce soul-language to subjective preference. It would examine the grammar of the doctrine within religious life.

5.4 Christianity, redemption, and the soul

Theologically sensitive interpretations often notice that Wittgenstein’s religious remarks are existential rather than doctrinal-systematic. He is deeply concerned with sin, guilt, confession, humility, pride, suffering, and the transformation of life. Italian theological-philosophical reception has emphasized this point: one source summarizes Wittgenstein’s view of Christianity as not primarily a doctrine or theory about what has happened or will happen to the human soul, but as a description of a real event in human life, tied to prayer and invocation.

That interpretation fits Wittgenstein’s broader method. Christianity, on this reading, is not a quasi-scientific theory about supernatural objects. It is a form of life and a conversion of the will. This does not make it less serious. It may make it more serious, because its seriousness lies in the transformation of life rather than in speculative explanation.

6. The systematic philosophy of mind extractable from Wittgenstein

What follows is the most coherent “system” one can extract without betraying Wittgenstein’s anti-systematic method.

6.1 Grammar before ontology

Before asking “What is the mind?” we must ask how mental concepts function. Philosophical ontology often goes wrong because it takes a word and demands an object corresponding to it. Wittgenstein’s method reverses the priority: grammar first, metaphysics later, if at all.

This does not make the inquiry superficial. Grammar, for Wittgenstein, is not school grammar. It is the structure of sense: what counts as using a word meaningfully, what counts as a criterion, what counts as doubt, what counts as explanation, what counts as a mistake.

6.2 The person is primitive

The basic subject of mental predicates is not an inner ego but the living person. Pain, fear, hope, remorse, belief, expectation, and understanding are ascribed to persons. Sometimes we speak of brains, bodies, faces, souls, minds, or hearts, but these uses are derivative, figurative, clinical, poetic, theological, or context-specific.

A Wittgensteinian philosophy of mind is therefore a philosophy of persons before it is a theory of mental objects.

This resembles some Aristotelian and Thomistic approaches, and that connection has been developed explicitly in French reception. Roger Pouivet’s French essay on Wittgenstein, the “inner man,” and the metaphysics of the soul notes his own larger project of reading Wittgenstein’s psychology as recovering aspects of Aristotelian-Thomistic philosophy of mind, in conversation with figures such as Peter Geach, Elizabeth Anscombe, and Anthony Kenny.

The affinity is not accidental. Wittgenstein and Aristotelian-Thomistic traditions both resist the idea that mind is an inner object attached to a body-machine. But Wittgenstein reaches this through grammar rather than scholastic metaphysics.

6.3 The mind is expressive, not hidden

The mental is not hidden behind expression in the way a coin is hidden in a box. In many cases, expression is not evidence for an independently identified inner state; it is the manifestation of that state.

This is the difference between a symptom and an expression. A fever may be evidence of infection. A cry of pain is not merely evidence of pain in that way. It is part of the natural and learned grammar of pain.

This view lets Wittgenstein avoid both Cartesianism and behaviorism. Against Cartesianism, the mind is not a private theatre. Against behaviorism, behavior is not a mere substitute for the mental. Expression is a category in its own right.

6.4 First-person authority is grammatical

The authority of “I am in pain” is not based on privileged inner perception. It is based on the grammar of avowal. In ordinary cases, I do not identify my pain by evidence. I express it.

This also explains why “I know I am in pain” can sound odd in ordinary contexts. Knowledge claims usually have a contrast with doubt, evidence, or possible error. But first-person present-tense psychological utterances often do not work that way.

This does not mean I am infallible about everything psychological. I can be wrong about my motives, self-deceived about my jealousy, confused about whether I am in love, mistaken about why I acted, or unable to name my mood. Wittgenstein’s point is not a blanket doctrine of infallibility. It is a differentiated grammar of psychological language.

6.5 Other minds are not theoretical posits

The other person is not normally known by an argument from analogy. I do not first observe a body, infer an inner mind, and then decide how to respond. I encounter a person.

This is why Wittgenstein says that his attitude toward the other is an attitude toward a soul. The “soul” is not a hypothesis added to bodily evidence. It is internal to the human attitude.

The skeptical problem of other minds arises when one abstracts from ordinary criteria and then demands a proof. Wittgenstein’s response is not to produce a better proof but to show that the demand has distorted the grammar of our relation to others.

6.6 Consciousness belongs to life

Consciousness is not an object but a family of concepts applied within the life of living beings. Being awake, aware, attentive, responsive, stunned, unconscious, dreaming, pretending, noticing, ignoring, and recognizing are not all one thing. They form overlapping language-games.

This is why Wittgenstein’s remark about living human beings matters. To call something conscious is not merely to attribute an inner glow. It is to place it within a grammar of life, responsiveness, expression, and possibility.

6.7 Inner episodes exist, but they do not fix meaning

Wittgenstein does not deny inner speech, mental imagery, moods, felt tensions, or sudden experiences of understanding. He denies that such episodes are the hidden foundation of meaning.

A person may have an image while understanding a word, but the image does not determine the word’s use. A person may feel confidence while following a rule, but the feeling does not constitute correct rule-following. A person may have an inner sentence before speaking, but the inner sentence is itself meaningful only within a language.

6.8 The mental is normative

To understand, intend, mean, believe, expect, and obey a rule is to be answerable to norms. One can misunderstand, misapply, misremember, deceive, pretend, fail, correct oneself, or be corrected by others.

This normativity cannot be derived from a private episode. If whatever seems right is right, the distinction between seeming right and being right disappears. That is the deep lesson of the private-language argument.

6.9 Neuroscience is legitimate but not conceptually sovereign

Wittgenstein’s view is compatible with empirical neuroscience, but it rejects the idea that neuroscience can replace the grammar of mental concepts. Brain processes are causally important. They may explain perception, memory, sleep, injury, aphasia, mood disorders, or attention. But the meaning of “pain,” “belief,” “hope,” “understanding,” or “soul” is not discovered simply by locating neural correlates.

The distinction is between causal explanation and conceptual clarification. Neuroscience may explain mechanisms. Philosophy clarifies the grammar of the concepts used to describe minded life. Confusing those projects produces category mistakes.

6.10 The soul is the person under an ethical-religious aspect

The word “soul” has different grammars. Sometimes it is used theologically: salvation of the soul, immortality of the soul, the soul before God. Sometimes ethically: a cruel soul, a noble soul, a wounded soul. Sometimes psychologically: a soul in torment. Sometimes aesthetically: the soul expressed in a face, gesture, or voice.

Wittgenstein’s late philosophy helps us see why these uses are not reducible to one metaphysical object. They are anchored in ways of taking human life. The soul is not a private item; it is a dimension of the person as expressively, ethically, and religiously encountered.

French Cavellian reception captures this well. A French essay on the private, the secret, and ordinary language reads Wittgenstein’s “human body is the best picture of the human soul” as meaning not that the body represents or possesses the soul as an object, but that it gives expression to it within a form of life.

7. Wittgenstein against major positions in philosophy of mind

Wittgenstein is best understood not as occupying one standard position, but as changing the terrain on which those positions are stated.

Against Cartesian dualism

Cartesian dualism treats the mind as an inner thinking substance. Wittgenstein rejects the model of the mind as a private object. The “I” does not name a hidden ego. Sensation words are not grounded by private ostension. The soul is not an occult thing behind the body.

Against reductive materialism

Reductive materialism often assumes that mental concepts must be identified with brain states if they are to be real. Wittgenstein would treat this as another grammatical pressure from a misleading picture. The reality of pain does not depend on pain being an inner object; neither does it depend on “pain” meaning a neural event. Brain events may be causally necessary for pain, but they are not automatically the grammar of pain.

Against logical behaviorism

Wittgenstein is often mistaken for a behaviorist because he emphasizes criteria and expression. But he explicitly does not deny mental processes. His point is that the philosophical picture of an “inner process” often misleads us about the use of psychological language. In the Investigations, he says he is not denying that there is a mental process, but rejecting the picture of an inner process as the correct account of the use of certain words.

Against functionalism as a complete theory

Functionalism defines mental states by causal role. Wittgenstein would agree that mental concepts are tied to roles in life, action, and response. But he would resist turning this into a hidden theoretical essence. The roles of psychological concepts are not all causal-computational. They are also expressive, normative, ethical, interpersonal, and grammatical.

Against qualia as private objects

Wittgenstein does not deny the felt character of experience. He attacks the idea that this felt character is a private object whose name is fixed by inner pointing. The modern “qualia” debate often revives the very picture he criticized: ineffable private items known by acquaintance. A Wittgensteinian response would not say “there is no experience”; it would ask what work the qualia-picture is doing and whether it preserves a real distinction or manufactures a pseudo-object.

Against eliminativism

Eliminativism says ordinary mental vocabulary may be discarded in favor of scientific vocabulary. Wittgenstein’s view points the other way. Ordinary psychological concepts are not primitive scientific theories awaiting replacement. They are woven into human life. To eliminate them would not be like replacing phlogiston; it would be more like trying to eliminate promising, forgiving, grieving, confessing, pretending, or comforting from human practices.

8. Theological and spiritual psychology: guilt, confession, prayer, redemption

The soul in Wittgenstein is not only a philosophy-of-mind term. It is also an ethical and religious term. This becomes especially clear when we consider guilt, confession, prayer, and redemption.

In the Lecture on Ethics, Wittgenstein treats guilt in explicitly religious grammar: the experience can be described as God’s disapproval. He also treats absolute safety as expressible through the picture of being safe in God’s hands.

Such remarks are not psychological reports in a neutral clinical register. They show that religious language can articulate an orientation of the whole person. “My soul is guilty,” “God sees me,” “I must confess,” “I am redeemed,” or “I am lost” are not ordinary empirical claims about inner objects. They are expressions embedded in a practice of self-understanding, judgment, worship, and transformation.

Spanish-language scholarship is useful here. Vicente Sanfélix Vidarte’s article “Un alma enferma” reads Wittgenstein’s religious experience through William James’s notion of the “sick soul,” arguing that Wittgenstein took from James a religious ideal that helps explain his biography, his hostility to bourgeois civilization, and his sense of philosophy as religiously significant.

This line of interpretation matters because it prevents an over-Anglophone narrowing of Wittgenstein into a merely technical philosopher of language. Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind is bound up with seriousness about life, sin, self-deception, pride, and the need for transformation. His remarks on “soul” should be read in that existential register as well as in the anti-Cartesian one.

9. Multilingual reception and why it matters

The Anglophone reception has been dominated by private language, rule-following, ordinary language philosophy, skepticism, and philosophy of psychology. Those are indispensable. But French, Spanish, Italian, and German discussions often foreground themes that Anglophone treatments can underemphasize: the body as expression of the soul, religious conversion, Thomistic affinities, the anthropology of the human being, and the spiritual seriousness of Wittgenstein’s method.

In French reception, two tendencies are especially relevant. One is Cavellian: the body gives expression to the soul, and skepticism about other minds is not merely epistemological but ethical. The other is Aristotelian-Thomistic: Wittgenstein’s anti-Cartesian philosophy of psychology can be read as recovering a non-dualist account of the human being. Pouivet’s French work explicitly places Wittgenstein in conversation with Anscombe, Geach, Kenny, and Thomistic philosophy of mind.

In Spanish reception, Sanfélix’s “sick soul” interpretation is particularly valuable because it treats Wittgenstein’s religiosity as central rather than eccentric. It connects philosophy, biography, Jamesian religious psychology, and critique of bourgeois modernity. Spanish-language work on Wittgenstein and the naturalization of mind also tends to emphasize that the critique of private language is a critique of modern philosophy’s conception of mind, not merely a narrow semantic argument.

In Italian theological-philosophical reception, Wittgenstein’s remarks on Christianity, prayer, and the soul are often read as a refusal to reduce faith to speculative doctrine. The Italian DISF treatment emphasizes Christianity not as a theory about future events of the soul, but as something enacted and recognized in life.

In German materials, the original phrase “Der menschliche Körper ist das beste Bild der menschlichen Seele” keeps the Körper/Seele relation vivid: body and soul are not being handled as “physical object” and “mental object” in a neat analytic taxonomy. The phrase is about the visibility, expression, and aspectual perception of the human being.

The multilingual lesson is this: Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind should not be extracted only as an argument against private language. It is also an anthropology, an ethics of acknowledgment, and a spiritually charged critique of the pictures by which modern people misunderstand themselves.

10. A consolidated Wittgensteinian account of soul, mind, and consciousness

Here is the most systematic reconstruction.

The mind is not a private arena. It is the complex grammar of a living person’s capacities, expressions, acts, sufferings, dispositions, avowals, perceptions, memories, intentions, and forms of responsiveness. Some of these involve inner episodes; many do not; none are made meaningful by private ostension alone.

Consciousness is not a luminous object inspected inwardly. It is a family of concepts applied to living beings within practices of recognition, attention, responsiveness, report, awareness, sleep, waking, sensation, and action. Consciousness-ascription is grammatically tied to life.

The self is not a hidden object named by “I.” In early Wittgenstein, the metaphysical subject is the limit of the world, not an item in it. In later Wittgenstein, the first-person pronoun has multiple uses, and its philosophical misuse creates the illusion of a private ego.

The soul is not best understood as an occult substance behind the body. Nor is it a meaningless leftover. It is the human being under the aspect of expressive, ethical, interpersonal, and religious significance. The soul is visible—not as an object seen behind the body, but in the living body, voice, gesture, face, action, suffering, and form of life.

Other minds are not inferred as hidden causes. They are encountered in expressive life. Skepticism about other minds arises when philosophers detach mental concepts from the criteria and attitudes that give them sense.

Religious soul-language is neither empirical psychology nor empty poetry. It is a serious grammar of human life before God: guilt, confession, redemption, immortality, judgment, prayer, hope, and transformation. Its meaning lies in its use within a religious form of life, not in its conversion into scientific hypothesis.

Philosophy of mind, for Wittgenstein, is not a science of hidden mechanisms. It is therapy for conceptual confusion. But that therapy has positive consequences: it gives us a non-Cartesian, non-reductive, embodied, expressive philosophy of the person.

11. Final synthesis: the soul as expressive human life under depth-aspect

The most important sentence for the whole topic is still: the human body is the best picture of the human soul. Properly understood, this sentence contains the core of Wittgenstein’s mature philosophy of mind.

It does not mean that the body is a picture of a hidden object. It means that the living body is the place where the soul is manifest. The face, voice, posture, wound, cry, silence, prayer, shame, tenderness, cruelty, and confession are not mere external signs attached to an invisible interior. They are the life of the person.

Wittgenstein’s philosophy of mind is therefore a philosophy of manifest interiority. What is “inner” is not abolished, but it is not hidden in the way philosophers imagine. It is expressed, lived, enacted, concealed, betrayed, confessed, and recognized. A person can hide pain, disguise intention, suppress grief, lie about belief, or be opaque to herself. But these complexities do not restore the Cartesian theatre. They belong to the grammar of human life.

The theological remarks deepen rather than distract from this. They show that human beings do not understand themselves only as organisms with mental states. They understand themselves as guilty or forgiven, lost or redeemed, safe in God’s hands or abandoned, mortal yet addressed by pictures of eternity. Wittgenstein does not turn these pictures into science. He asks what they do in a life.

The resulting philosophy is austere but humane: do not invent occult objects; do not flatten persons into behavior; do not confuse scientific explanation with conceptual grammar; do not dismiss religious pictures merely because they are not empirical hypotheses; and do not look behind the human being for the soul when the living human being is already its best picture.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
